ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for packaged mixed vegetable juice; SNOMED CT coverage for commercial beverage subcategories is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of packaged vegetable juice is approximately 250 ml (one standard carton or bottle), equivalent to about 250 g.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetable juice, canned or bottled — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11540)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D03040025, confidence 0.95). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for vegetable juice cocktail (NDB 11540) to validate macro plausibility. HPB values used as primary. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy (0.01 g/100 g) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. Calcium and iron reported as 0.0 mg per HPB source — confirmed trace/negligible per USDA cross-reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for generic packaged mixed vegetable juice in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 43 is derived from the closest analogous entry: tomato juice (GI ~38, University of Sydney) and carrot juice (GI ~43, Atkinson et al. 2008). A blended vegetable juice GI of 43 (low category) is used as a conservative estimate. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (43 × 7.83 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2.5) / 100 = 8.4. Input record cited GI 15 with confidence 0.346 from an unspecified embedded database — this value is not supported by an approved GI source and has been replaced with the analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar content 7.37 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold for beverages; sodium is low at 32 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Although sodium is very low (32 mg/100 g), the sugar content of 7.37 g/100 g classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage, which independently raises blood pressure risk (AHA 2018); individuals with hypertension should limit intake to no more than 100–150 ml per occasion and verify whether sugars are naturally occurring from vegetables or added.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (7.37 g/100 g) with low fibre (1.22 g/100 g) in liquid form accelerates glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Packaged vegetable juice delivers 7.37 g sugar per 100 ml in liquid form with only 1.22 g/100 g fibre, resulting in rapid glucose absorption and blood glucose elevation; individuals with type 2 diabetes should strongly prefer whole vegetables, and if consuming this juice, limit to 100–150 ml paired with a protein-rich food to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sugar content 7.37 g/100 g in liquid form; fat is negligible (0.05 g/100 g) and sodium is low (32 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While fat and sodium are negligible, the sugar content of 7.37 g/100 g in a liquid beverage contributes to elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake and choose unsweetened, whole-food vegetable sources instead.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar (7.37 g/100 g) is pro-inflammatory; processing removes most polyphenols and fibre
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Packaged vegetable juice lacks meaningful anti-inflammatory factors (no omega-3, polyphenols, or >3 g whole-food fibre per 100 g) and its concentrated sugar content is pro-inflammatory; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should opt for freshly pressed, unsweetened vegetable juice or whole vegetables and check labels for added sugars.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Packaged vegetable juice (any flavour) is a commercially processed, ready-to-drink beverage made from a blend of multiple vegetables such as tomatoes, carrots, celery, beets, and leafy greens. The juice is extracted, pasteurised, and sealed in cartons, bottles, or cans for retail sale. It has a savoury-to-mildly-sweet flavour profile depending on the vegetable blend, with a smooth, thin consistency. Per 100 ml it provides approximately 32 kcal, 0.65 g protein, 0.05 g fat, 7.83 g carbohydrate (of which 7.37 g is sugar), 1.22 g dietary fibre, and 32 mg sodium. Potassium is moderate (~162 mg/100 ml), and small amounts of vitamin C (~8.4 mg) and vitamin A (~45 mcg RAE) are present. Calcium and iron are negligible (confirmed trace per HPB and USDA sources). The glycemic index is estimated at 43 (low category) based on analogous vegetable juice entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; glycemic load per 250 ml serving is approximately 8.4. Clinically, the sugar content of 7.37 g/100 ml classifies this product as a sugar-sweetened beverage under standard thresholds (>5 g/100 ml), which is relevant for hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease management. Whole vegetables are strongly preferred over packaged juice for all three conditions. The product is suitable for vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free diets. It is widely available in Singapore and globally as a convenient, low-calorie beverage option.
common_user_queries: Is packaged vegetable juice good for diabetics?
How many calories in vegetable juice?
Is vegetable juice high in sugar?
Can I drink vegetable juice if I have high blood pressure?
Is vegetable juice better than fruit juice?
What is the glycemic index of vegetable juice?
Is vegetable juice good for weight loss?
Does vegetable juice count as a serving of vegetables?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D03040025, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for plausibility. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (peer-reviewed), replacing the unsupported embedded GI of 15.
notes: 1. Calcium and iron are reported as 0.0 mg per HPB source; confirmed as trace/negligible per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cross-reference (NDB 11540). These are confirmed zero values, not missing data. 2. Saturated fat (0.01 g/100 g) estimated from USDA SR Legacy as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient breakdown not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry, and USDA SR Legacy does not provide a sufficiently close match for all micronutrients of a generic multi-vegetable blend. 4. Input record GI value of 15 (confidence 0.346, source: Embedded GI Database) was not supported by an approved GI source and has been replaced with an analogous estimate of GI 43 from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. 5. This food is a generic commercial category record; actual nutrient values may vary by brand and vegetable blend. 6. The food is not cuisine-specific (Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese), so no mandatory local-language names apply under G6 multilingual rules. Local name fields are set to null accordingly.

improvement_actions_taken: Replaced unsupported GI value of 15 (embedded database, confidence 0.346) with analogous estimate of 43 from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008.
Added saturated_fat_g (0.01) estimated from USDA SR Legacy NDB 11540 as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown.
Added potassium_mg (162.0), vitamin_c_mg (8.4), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (45.0), cholesterol_mg (0.0), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.0), vitamin_d_mcg (0.0) from USDA SR Legacy cross-reference.
Documented all -1 sentinel fields and confirmed-zero fields in notes per G3 and g3 rules.
Confirmed no mandatory multilingual names required for generic commercial beverage under G6.
Populated full health_context with Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and Cardiovascular Disease entries with clinical guidance.
Built comprehensive aliases and aliases_by_language with consistency check performed.
Added FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology anchors with honest gap note for SNOMED CT.
